mnemonics.title: Linked List Parts
mnemonics.content: NODE = DATA + LINK: Every node has DATA for storing info and LINK (pointer) to next node in chain.

mnemonics.title: Operations Complexity
mnemonics.content: HEAD OPERATIONS ARE FAST (O1), TAIL OPERATIONS ARE SLOW (On): Insert/delete at beginning is constant time, but at end requires traversal.

mnemonics.title: Linked List vs Array
mnemonics.content: ARRAYS ARE CONTIGUOUS, LINKED LISTS ARE SCATTERED: Arrays need consecutive memory, linked lists can use any available memory locations.

mnemonics.title: Doubly Linked List
mnemonics.content: DOUBLY HAS TWO WAYS TO PLAY: Each node has PREV and NEXT pointers, allowing forward and backward traversal.

acronyms.expansion: Linked List - Linear data structure with nodes containing data and pointer to next node
acronyms.usage: Used to represent dynamic collections where size changes frequently

acronyms.expansion: Singly Linked List - Each node has only a next pointer, unidirectional traversal
acronyms.usage: Used when only forward traversal is needed and memory is constrained

acronyms.expansion: Doubly Linked List - Each node has prev and next pointers, bidirectional traversal
acronyms.usage: Used when bidirectional traversal or reverse iteration is required

acronyms.expansion: Circular Linked List - Last node points back to head, no NULL termination
acronyms.usage: Used for round-robin scheduling, circular buffers, and problems requiring cyclic iteration

acronyms.expansion: First In First Out - Queue behavior, opposite of stack
acronyms.usage: Queues implemented via linked lists follow FIFO principle

visualTips.visualization: Draw each node as a box split into two halves: LEFT HALF = DATA FIELD, RIGHT HALF = POINTER FIELD (arrow pointing right). Multiple nodes connected by arrows forming a chain.
Imagine walking through a train - each coach (node) connects to the next, you must pass through each coach to reach the end. Start at engine (head) and follow connections.
Think of adding a new car at the FRONT of a train: place new car, connect it to the current first car, then make it the new engine (head).
Like attaching a new coach at the END of a train: find the last coach, connect new coach to it, update the end marker (tail).
Removing a train car: disconnect it from both sides, connect previous car directly to next car, then remove the disconnected car.

analogies.analogy: Think of array as a ROW OF PARKING SPOTS (contiguous, numbered) where you can directly go to spot #50. Linked list is like a TREASURE HUNT where each clue (node) tells you where the next clue is located - you must follow the chain.
Pointers are like ADDRESSES IN A CITY. Each house (node) has a note saying "next house at: [address]". To find all houses, you follow these address directions.
Arrays are like RESERVING AN ENTIRE HOTEL FLOOR at booking time (fixed), while linked lists are like CHECKING INTO ROOMS AS THEY BECOME AVAILABLE (dynamic allocation).
Each node is like a TRAIN CARRIAGE: it carries cargo (data) and has a connector (pointer) to attach to the next carriage.
The head pointer is like the ADDRESS OF THE FIRST PAGE in a chain of linked notes. Without it, you cannot find any note in the chain.
